sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=30, col=36).
You have progressed to a new level!
Current state: step 67, level 2.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'MOUSE': 'Clicking object center moves white diamond there and shifts clicked object along path toward next object. Oscillating between magenta center [56,27] and dark gray diamond [60,27] is the current mechanic.'}
- Object taxonomy: {'purple_ring': 'hollow diamond ring at ~21,39', 'white_diamond': 'white diamond that follows clicks, moves to clicked position', 'gray_path': 'reconfigurable diagonal path', 'purple_square': 'purple square with magenta center at [56,27]', 'dark_gray_diamond': 'dark gray diamond at [60,27]'}
- Win pattern: HYPOTHESIS: Continue oscillating clicks until level completes
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the data:
- Key movable objects (12-20 pixels with children):
  - W (white): (6, 17) - this is the cursor
  - G (gray): (9, 49), (21, 8), (35, 45), (48, 54) - gray diamonds
  - O (orange): (12, 24) - orange square
  - p (purple): (41, 49) - purple square
- Rings:
  - Orange ring: (12, 24) - but wait, this is the same as the orange square!
  - Purple ring: (41, 49) - same as purple square!
So the 20-pixel objects ARE the rings with objects inside them. Let me reconsider.
Looking at the image again:
- Top left: white diamond cursor at (6, 17)
- Orange square at (12, 24) - this is a SOLID orange object (20 pixels with child)
- Gray diamonds at (9, 49), (21, 8), (35, 45), (48, 54)
- Purple square at (41, 49) - SOLID purple object (20 pixels with child)
- Purple ring (hollow) at top right
- Orange ring (hollow) at bottom
Wait, the output said "orange ring: center=(12,24)" and "purple ring: center=(41,49)" but those are the solid squares. The hollow rings must be different objects.
Let me look at the image more carefully:
- There's a purple hollow ring at approximately (20, 57)
- There's an orange hollow ring at approximately (52, 40)
The 20-pixel objects with children are the SOLID squares that need to be moved:
- Orange square at (12, 24) → needs to go to orange ring
- Purple square at (41, 49) → needs to go to purple ring
The gray diamonds (12 pixels each) also need to be moved. They might need to go to specific positions.
From level 1, the mechanic was: click an object to move it one step toward the ring. I need to figure out which objects go where.
Looking at the image layout:
- Orange square (12, 24) is near top left, orange ring is at bottom center
- Purple square (41, 49) is near bottom right, purple ring is at top right
- Gray diamonds are scattered
The white paths show connections:
- Orange square connects to gray diamond at (9, 49) via white path
- Purple square connects to gray diamond at (35, 45) via white path
Wait, let me reconsider. The gray diamonds might be the ones that need to move to the rings, while the colored squares are the rings themselves.
Actually no - the colored squares have 20 pixels (larger) and the gray diamonds have 12 pixels (smaller). In level 1, the purple square (20 pixels) moved to the ring. So the 20-pixel objects are the ones that move.
But the output shows orange ring at (12, 24) which is the orange square's position. This is confusing.
Let me look at the image again:
- There IS a hollow purple ring at top right (around row 20, col 57)
- There IS a hollow orange ring at bottom (around row 52, col 40)
- The orange square at (12, 24) is a SOLID object
- The purple square at (41, 49) is a SOLID object
The code identified the 20-pixel objects as "rings" because I searched for p==20. But those are actually the solid squares. The hollow rings would have different pixel counts.
Let me search for the hollow rings properly. They should be made of individual 1-pixel segments forming a diamond shape.
Looking at the image:
- Purple ring: individual purple pixels forming a diamond at ~20, 57
- Orange ring: individual orange pixels forming a diamond at ~52, 40
From the earlier full output, I saw many 1-pixel 'p' objects around (15-21, 54-60) which form the purple ring, and many 1-pixel 'O' objects around (48-54, 37-43) which form the orange ring.
So the task is:
1. Move orange square (12, 24) to orange ring (~52, 40)
2. Move purple square (41, 49) to purple ring (~20, 57)
3. Move gray diamonds to... somewhere?
Let me start by clicking the gray diamonds first since they're on the paths, then the colored squares.
Actually, from level 1, I learned that clicking the object directly moves it. Let me try clicking the gray diamond at (48, 54) first since it's farthest from any ring.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Identify hollow rings (made of 1-pixel segments forming diamond shapes)
seg = current_frame.segmentation

# Find purple ring center (individual p pixels around top right)
p_pixels = [(n['boundary'][0][0], n['boundary'][0][1]) for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'p' and n['pixels'] == 1]
if p_pixels:
    pr = sum(r for r, c in p_pixels) / len(p_pixels)
    pc = sum(c for r, c in p_pixels) / len(p_pixels)
    print(f"Purple ring center: ({pr:.0f}, {pc:.0f})")

# Find orange ring center (individual O pixels around bottom)
o_pixels = [(n['boundary'][0][0], n['boundary'][0][1]) for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'O' and n['pixels'] == 1]
if o_pixels:
    orr = sum(r for r, c in o_pixels) / len(o_pixels)
    orc = sum(c for r, c in o_pixels) / len(o_pixels)
    print(f"Orange ring center: ({orr:.0f}, {orc:.0f})")

# Movable objects
print("\nMovable objects:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    c, p = n['color'], n['pixels']
    if p in (12, 20) and n['children']:
        b = n['boundary']
        r = (min(x[0] for x in b) + max(x[0] for x in b))//2
        cc = (min(x[1] for x in b) + max(x[1] for x in b))//2
        print(f"  {c} ({p}px): ({r},{cc})")

# Strategy: click gray diamonds first (they're on paths), then colored squares
# Start with gray diamond at (48, 54) - farthest from rings
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 48, 'col': 54}])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"MOUSE": "Clicking an object moves it one step along path toward ring target"}, "object_taxonomy": {"purple_ring": "hollow ring top right", "orange_ring": "hollow ring bottom", "gray_diamond": "movable 12px object", "purple_square": "movable 20px object", "orange_square": "movable 20px object", "white_diamond": "cursor"}, "win_pattern": "Click each object to move it to matching ring", "level_log": {"level_1": "Completed in 4 clicks"}, "cross_level_notes": "Click objects to move them along paths to rings"}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Level 2: gray diamonds and colored squares need to reach rings", "goal_model": "Move all objects to their rings", "recent_findings": ["Orange ring ~52,40, purple ring ~20,57"], "open_questions": ["Which gray diamonds go to which rings?"], "current_plan": "Click gray diamonds then colored squares", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Purple ring center: (21, 56)
Orange ring center: (43, 37)

Movable objects:
  W (12px): (6,17)
  G (12px): (9,49)
  O (20px): (12,24)
  G (12px): (21,8)
  G (12px): (35,45)
  p (20px): (41,49)
  G (12px): (48,54)
